description: Scheduler kernel functions for CPU and latency observability

answers: which process is being scheduled on/off CPU
probes.use_when: you suspect CPU scheduling issues — a process spending too long in the runqueue, or being preempted too frequently. very high frequency — always sample.
probes.not_when: you need overall CPU utilization — use /proc/stat or cgroup metrics for that.

interpretations.pattern: frequent switches between same two processes
interpretations.conclusion: CPU contention between two processes — they're fighting for the same core
interpretations.action: check CPU affinity and cgroup CPU limits. consider spreading workloads across cores.

answers: which processes are being woken up
is a process waiting too long to be scheduled

fields.name: pid
fields.meaning: PID of the process being woken

probes.use_when: you suspect scheduling latency — a process is being woken but taking too long to get CPU time. very high frequency — always use sampling (sample_rate: 0.01).
probes.not_when: you need CPU utilization metrics — use /proc/stat or cgroup metrics.

interpretations.pattern: high wakeup rate for a specific process
interpretations.conclusion: process is frequently sleeping and waking — may be waiting on I/O or locks
interpretations.action: normal for I/O-bound processes. if latency is high, check what the process is blocking on.

interpretations.pattern: wakeup events without corresponding task switch
interpretations.conclusion: process was woken but did not get scheduled — runqueue congestion
interpretations.action: too many runnable processes for available CPUs. check node CPU pressure and pod scheduling.
